summary:
The Mughal Empire (1526–1857 AD) was the dominant power of the Indian subcontinent for three centuries, ruling at its height over most of what is now India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan, and creating a sophisticated syncretic civilization that fused Persian, Timurid, and Indian cultural traditions into one of the wealthiest and most culturally productive empires in world history. Founded by [[event:2512|Babur]] after his victory at the [[event:2380|First Battle of Panipat]] in 1526, the empire was consolidated by [[event:3233|Humayun]] (with a notable interruption by [[event:3234|Sher Shah Suri]]) and reached its administrative and cultural peak under [[event:2381|Akbar the Great]] (r. 1556–1605), who created a centralized revenue administration, pursued a policy of religious tolerance that incorporated Hindu Rajput nobles into the ruling class, and patronized an extraordinary flourishing of art, architecture, and literature.

The Mughal golden age under Akbar, Jahangir, and [[event:2506|Shah Jahan]] produced architectural masterpieces — the [[event:2382|Taj Mahal]], the Red Fort, Fatehpur Sikri — a refinement of [[event:3505|miniature painting]], rich court poetry in Persian and Urdu, and administrative systems that generated revenues comparable to the entire European economy. At its peak under [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]] (r. 1658–1707), the empire's nominal territory reached its maximum extent, but Aurangzeb's reversal of Akbar's tolerance policies, reimposition of the jizya tax on non-Muslims, and destruction of Hindu temples fueled the [[event:2368|Maratha]] revolt and Sikh resistance that would unravel Mughal power. After Aurangzeb's death in 1707, the [[event:2446|rapid decline]] began: provincial governors became effectively independent, the Persians under [[event:2447|Nader Shah]] sacked Delhi in 1739, and the [[event:2368|Marathas]] came to dominate central India, leaving the Mughals as ceremonial figureheads until the British formally dissolved the dynasty after the 1857 uprising.
